scripts.title: It's not the ad that's broken
scripts.film: Sit down, natural light, direct to camera. Relaxed and confident — like you've diagnosed this a hundred times. Lean in slightly on the 24-hour point.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service businesses fix the ad when the problem is everything that happens after the click.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
reach. click. form fill. call. booked job.

that's the chain.

most business owners only ever look at the first link.

but if a lead sits uncontacted for 24 hours — that's where you're bleeding.

before you change anything, check your missed calls.

captions.label: C
captions.text: check your uncontacted leads before you touch the campaign.

lines.text:
Your Meta campaign isn't underperforming because the ad is bad.

The leak is almost always somewhere else.

Think of it like a chain.

Reach. Click. Form fill. Call. Booked job.

Most business owners only fix the first link.

But if a lead sits uncontacted for 24 hours — that's where you're losing.

Before you change anything, check your missed calls.
Fix the chain. Not the ad.

scripts.title: Turning your ads on and off is killing your momentum
scripts.film: Sit down, calm, matter-of-fact. You've seen this exact mistake dozens of times. Deliver it like a simple fact, not a lecture.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
every time you pause your campaign, the algorithm forgets everything it learned.

you start from zero.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
the service businesses flooded with leads right now started running ads six weeks ago — when they didn't even need them.

that's not luck.

the algorithm builds momentum over time.

you can't build that in a campaign you keep turning off.

captions.label: C
captions.text: run ads when you're busy. that's when the algorithm learns best.

lines.text:
Every time you pause your Meta campaign because you're busy — you lose every bit of data the algorithm built.

You start from scratch.

The algorithm needs time and consistency to learn who your buyer is.

The longer it runs without being touched, the better and cheaper it gets.

Pause it and that learning disappears.

You don't have to spend more when you're busy — just reduce the budget and keep it alive.

Always-on doesn't mean always expensive.

It means always building.

scripts.title: The first five words of your ad are doing all the damage

overlay.line2: of your ad are the whole game.

scripts.film: Sit down, punchy energy, direct to camera. Short and sharp — this one moves fast. Lean in on the plumber example.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
nobody's scrolling looking for your business.

your hook has to make them feel seen before they know what you do.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
"we're your local plumber" — dead in one second.

"water pressure dropped in your home?" — stops the scroll.

same business. same offer. completely different result.

specificity is what earns the next 30 seconds.

captions.label: C
captions.text: lead with their situation. not yours.

lines.text:
Your ad is losing people before they've even processed what you do.

And it's almost always the first line.

Nobody's scrolling looking for you.

Your ad interrupts them mid-feed.

So the first thing you say has to make them think — that's me.

Not 'ABC Plumbing, local and reliable.'

Something like 'water pressure dropped in your home?' — that stops them.

Lead with their situation. Not yours.

You have one second.

Use it to talk about them.

scripts.title: Your phone has better ad creative than anything you paid for

overlay.line2: that make you look like everyone else.

scripts.film: Sit down or stand, relaxed, natural light. Hold your phone up briefly when you mention shooting on the job. Genuine energy, not hype.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
ran the same offer with a stock photo and a real job photo.

the real one got twice the clicks for less than half the cost.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
people know a stock photo in under a second.

it tells them: generic. skip.

but a real before-and-after from a real job — that stops them.

because it could be their house.

authenticity converts. stock photos don't.

captions.label: C
captions.text: 90 seconds on every job. that's your whole creative library.

lines.text:
I ran the same ad with a stock photo and a real job photo.

The real one got twice the clicks at less than half the cost.

People clock a stock photo before they've even processed it.

Brain says: generic. Skip.

But a real before-and-after — a real job that could be their house — that stops them.

You don't need a photographer.

You need one habit: phone out on every job, shoot the before and after.

That library is free. And it compounds.

Shoot the job.

Every single one.

scripts.title: Your ad didn't stop working. Your audience ran out.
scripts.film: Sit down, direct to camera, matter-of-fact. Simple diagnosis — deliver it cleanly. Mention Ads Manager by name.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
if your ads spike and die every few days, it's not the algorithm.

it's ad fatigue. and it's completely fixable.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
meta shows your ad to the same pool of people.

small audience, high frequency — they've seen it five times.

they stopped responding.

go check your frequency in ads manager.

if it's above 2.5 in week one, that's your answer.

captions.label: C
captions.text: check frequency before you change anything else.

lines.text:
Your ads peaked after three days and then died.

That's not bad luck. That's ad fatigue.

Meta shows your ad to the same pool of people.

Small audience, high frequency — they've seen it five times.

They stopped responding.

Go into Ads Manager and check your frequency column.

If it's above 2.5 in week one — that's your answer.

Either widen the audience or rotate new creative.

Check frequency first.

The answer is almost always right there.

scripts.title: The Follow-Up Window (Why Your Leads Go Cold)
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVHAaPz30=/. Start at the header, walk cursor left to right through the four circles — pause on each one. Slow down on 24 HOURS. Then move down to the fix and the callout box. Voice stays calm and direct — like this is something they should already know but probably don't.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your leads aren't going cold. you're just calling them too late.

captions.label: B
captions.text: someone fills in your form because they're thinking about their problem right now. wait 5 minutes — high chance they book. wait an hour — interest has already dropped. wait 24 hours — they've already called someone else. the follow-up window is real and most service businesses completely miss it.

captions.label: C
captions.text: call within 5 minutes. every time. no exceptions.

lines.text: Your Meta ads are probably working. Your follow-up is what's killing the leads.

The moment someone fills in your form, they are at peak interest. They just saw your ad, recognised their problem, and made a decision to reach out. That window is tiny. Call them inside five minutes and your chance of booking is extremely high. That's consistent across service businesses — the faster the response, the higher the close rate.

Wait one hour and that chance has already dropped significantly. Wait 24 hours and you've lost them — not because they forgot you, but because your competitor who called at the five-minute mark got the job. This is why business owners say their leads are low quality. The leads weren't low quality. The follow-up was slow.
Build the system. Five minutes, every time — and watch what happens to your close rate.

scripts.title: You're Targeting Too Broad (This Is Why Your Ads Waste Money)
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/BOARD_2_ID=/. Walk the wrong side first — point at each element and name it. Then move to the right side and walk through exactly what to change. Cursor deliberate, pause so viewers can read each point. Voice confident, like you've fixed this exact problem a hundred times.

captions.label: A
captions.text: broad targeting isn't reaching more people. it's paying to reach the wrong ones.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service businesses set meta targeting to everyone in the city aged 18-65 and wonder why leads are terrible. the algorithm needs a specific signal to find the right buyer. here's exactly how to give it one.

captions.label: C
captions.text: the tighter the targeting, the cheaper the leads.

lines.text: If your Meta ads are reaching everyone, they're converting no one. Here's exactly why.

Broad targeting means you're paying to show your ad to people who will never hire you. A plumber reaching every adult in the city is paying for renters, apartment dwellers, people who handle everything themselves. You're not buying leads — you're buying noise. Meta's algorithm needs a specific signal to find the right person. Give it a wide net and it guesses.

The fix is specificity. Who actually hires you — homeowners, certain suburbs, specific demographics, people who've already shown interest in home services. Narrow that down and Meta starts finding people who look like your actual customers. Cost per lead drops. Lead quality goes up. The same budget does more work.
Less reach, better leads. That trade is always worth making.

scripts.title: What 'cost per lead' actually means
scripts.film: Sit down, natural light, direct to camera. Calm and matter-of-fact — most business owners have never heard this before.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
low cost per lead doesn't mean the campaign is working.

low cost per booked job does.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
most service businesses judge their campaigns on cost per lead.

but a cheap lead that never picks up, never books, never converts — it cost you more than an expensive one that did.

the only number that actually matters is cost per booked job.

captions.label: C
captions.text: judge the campaign on booked jobs. not leads.

lines.text:
Everyone obsesses over cost per lead.

But that number on its own tells you almost nothing.

A cheap lead that never answers, never books, never converts — it cost you more than an expensive lead that did.

The metric that actually matters is cost per booked job.

A strong close rate can make expensive leads profitable.

A weak follow-up process bleeds out on cheap ones.
Know your numbers before you judge the campaign.

scripts.title: The offer was the problem — not the ad
scripts.film: Sit down, direct to camera. Sharp energy — this one challenges what most people think. Deliver it like a reality check.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
the ad gets people to look.

the offer gets them to act.

most businesses only ever fix the ad.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
changing your creative 10 times and getting the same result means the creative was never the problem.

if someone can't immediately see why they should choose you over anyone else — fix the offer first.

then run the ad.

captions.label: C
captions.text: you can't out-advertise a weak offer.

lines.text:
You've tested five creatives. Changed the copy. Tried new images.

Leads still aren't coming. The ad isn't the problem — the offer is.

An ad can only amplify what's already there.

If the reason someone should call you over anyone else isn't immediately obvious — no creative testing fixes that.

Before you touch the campaign, ask yourself: why should someone choose me specifically?

If that answer isn't clear, start there.

Fix the offer first.

Then the ad has something to work with.

scripts.title: Your competitors aren't better — they're just more visible
scripts.film: Sit down, natural light, calm and confident. Reality check energy — not a lecture. Easy and direct.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
the business getting all the leads in your area isn't better at the job.

they're just harder to ignore.

follow @marcomarkets if you run a service business and want to scale past $5k–$50k a month with meta ads

captions.label: B
captions.text:
visibility compounds.

six months of consistent ads and people already know your name before they call.

they trust you before the first conversation.

that's not luck — that's what showing up every week builds.

follow @marcomarkets if you run a service business and want to scale past $5k–$50k a month with meta ads

captions.label: C
captions.text:
they're not beating you on skill.

they're beating you on consistency.

lines.text:
The business in your area getting all the enquiries isn't better than you at the job.

They're just more visible.

Visibility compounds.

Six months of consistent ads and people already know your name before they call.

They trust you before the first conversation.

That doesn't come from one campaign.

It comes from showing up consistently, week after week.

The best time to start was six months ago.

Second best time is now.

scripts.title: Why a smaller audience gets more leads
scripts.film: Sit down, natural light, direct to camera. Let the counterintuitive point land — don't rush it.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
a broad audience doesn't mean more leads.

it means more budget spent on people who'll never call you.

captions.label: B
captions.text:
when your targeting is too wide, meta guesses who to show your ad to — and it usually guesses wrong.

narrow it to the right suburbs, the right homeowner profile, people who've shown interest.

meta finds your buyer faster and cheaper every time.

follow @marcomarkets if you run a service business and want to scale past $5k–$50k a month with meta ads

captions.label: C
captions.text: tight targeting, cheaper leads. every time.

lines.text:
More people seeing your ad doesn't mean more leads.

It usually means more budget wasted on people who'd never hire you.

When your audience is too wide, Meta has to guess who your buyer is.

And it usually guesses wrong.

Narrow it down — right suburbs, right homeowner profile, people who've already shown interest.

Meta has a clear signal to work with.

It finds your buyer faster and spends less doing it.

Reach fewer people.

Reach the right ones.

scripts.title: Why boosting a post is the most expensive mistake you can make

overlay.line1: the most expensive button on instagram.

scripts.film: Sit or stand, sharp energy. You've explained this a hundred times and it still surprises people. Keep it punchy — wake-up call energy.

captions.label: A
captions.text:
a boosted post reaches people who already follow you.

an ad reaches people who've never heard of you but need you right now.

follow @marcomarkets if you run a service business and want to scale past $5k–$50k a month with meta ads

captions.label: B
captions.text:
when you hit boost, meta shows your content to your existing audience — not new customers.

ads manager lets you target homeowners in specific areas, people actively searching, people who've visited your site.

completely different result.

DM me "ads" if you want to see what this looks like for your business.

captions.label: C
captions.text:
the problem usually isn't the budget.

it's where you're spending it.

lines.text:
The boost button feels like marketing.

It isn't.

When you boost, Meta shows your content to people who already follow you.

No new customers.

Nobody who's never heard of you.

Real campaigns in Ads Manager target by intent — homeowners in the right areas, people actively looking.

That's where new business comes from.

Stop boosting.

Start building campaigns.

scripts.title: The 3-Second Hook Formula (Why Most Ads Die Immediately)
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/BOARD_3_ID=/. Walk through each section top to bottom — pause cursor on each box, read the key point, then move on. Calm and methodical. The board is doing the visual teaching. Your voice is the commentary.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your ad has 3 seconds. most service businesses waste all three.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most ads die in the first 3 seconds — not because the offer is bad, but because the hook doesn't stop anyone. the formula is simple: pattern interrupt, one piece of real value, one clear action. most businesses aren't doing any of them right.

captions.label: C
captions.text: 3 seconds to earn the next 27.

lines.text: Your ad has three seconds before someone scrolls past it forever. Here's the exact formula for making those three seconds count.

Seconds one to three — the pattern interrupt. You need to say or show something that makes their thumb stop. Not your logo. Not your company name. Something that speaks directly to a problem they're already living. A plumber who opens with 'water bill gone up lately?' stops more scrolls than 'ABC Plumbing, serving your area.' Start with them. Not you.

Seconds four to twenty — one clear piece of value. Not three tips. Not a full service rundown. One insight that makes them think: I didn't know that, or — that's exactly my problem. The moment someone learns something from your ad, you become the authority. That's when trust begins and they keep watching.

Last ten seconds — one clear next step. Not 'visit the website' and 'give us a call' and 'follow for more tips.' One action. The more options you give them, the less likely they are to do any of them. Pick the one you want and make it as easy as possible to take.
Interrupt. Deliver value. Direct to one action. That's it.

scripts.title: Why retargeting is your cheapest lead source
scripts.film: Screen record Meta Ads Manager. Start on a campaign dashboard. Navigate to show a retargeting audience vs a cold traffic audience — highlight the size difference. Then move to Results and compare cost per lead between a cold campaign and a retargeting campaign side by side. Keep cursor intentional, zoom in on the key numbers, speak naturally over what you're showing.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your cheapest leads aren't cold traffic. they're the people who already showed interest and didn't book.

captions.label: B
captions.text: retargeting works because the hard part is already done — they already know your business, they already showed interest. you're not convincing a stranger. you're just giving a warm lead one more reason to act. and it costs a fraction of cold traffic every time.

captions.label: C
captions.text: cold traffic fills the funnel. retargeting converts it.

lines.text: If you're spending your entire budget on cold traffic and barely running retargeting — you're doing it backwards.

Look at this cold traffic campaign. New people, never heard of the business. Cost per lead is decent but you're working hard to get there. Meta has to show the ad multiple times before anyone converts because there's zero prior trust. These people don't know your work, haven't seen your results, have no reason to choose you over anyone else.

Now look at the retargeting campaign. Same business, same offer. But these are people who visited the website, saw a post, maybe even filled in a form and didn't book. They're warm. The trust is already there. You're not convincing anyone — you're just showing up again at the right moment and reminding them why they were interested in the first place.

Cost per lead from retargeting is almost always lower. You're paying to close, not to introduce. And the quality is higher because these people already chose to engage with your business. That's where your ad budget works the hardest — and most service businesses completely ignore it.
Cold traffic builds the funnel. Retargeting converts it. Run both.
